indication: New diagnosis of persistent atrial fibrillation, evaluate for left atrial appendage thrombus prior to initiation of anticoagulation and consideration of cardioversion
technique: Standard transesophageal echocardiographic examination was performed using a multiplane transesophageal probe after adequate sedation and topical pharyngeal anesthesia. The patient tolerated the procedure well without complications.

sedation.monitoring: Continuous pulse oximetry, cardiac monitoring, and blood pressure monitoring

leftAtrium.appendage: Left atrial appendage is dilated. No thrombus identified. LAA emptying velocity 32 cm/s (mildly reduced).
leftAtrium.spontaneousEchoContrast: Mild spontaneous echo contrast ('smoke') present in the left atrium, indicating low flow and elevated thromboembolic risk.

findings.interatrialSeptum: No patent foramen ovale or atrial septal defect identified by color flow Doppler. Agitated saline contrast injection shows no right-to-left shunt.
findings.leftVentricle: Mildly dilated. Estimated EF 35-40%. Hypokinesis of the inferior and inferolateral walls.
findings.rightSide: Right atrium is mildly dilated. Right ventricle is normal in size with preserved function.
findings.mitralValve: Mild-to-moderate mitral regurgitation (2-3+). Leaflets are mildly thickened. No prolapse. No vegetation. No stenosis. Regurgitation is central, consistent with functional etiology.
findings.aorticValve: Trileaflet. Mild sclerosis without stenosis. No regurgitation. No vegetation.
findings.tricuspidValve: Trace-to-mild tricuspid regurgitation. Estimated RVSP 32 mmHg.

aorta.descendingAorta: Mild atherosclerotic plaque in the descending thoracic aorta. No mobile or protruding atheroma.

impression: No left atrial appendage thrombus identified.
Mild spontaneous echo contrast in the left atrium, indicating low flow state and elevated thromboembolic risk.
Moderately-to-severely dilated left atrium with reduced LAA emptying velocities.
Mildly dilated left ventricle with mildly-to-moderately reduced systolic function (EF 35-40%) and inferior/inferolateral wall motion abnormalities.
No intracardiac source of embolism aside from the left atrial low flow state.
Mild descending thoracic aortic atherosclerosis without mobile atheroma.
From a TEE standpoint, cardioversion may be considered if clinically indicated.
recommendations: Initiate anticoagulation for stroke prevention given CHA2DS2-VASc score
If cardioversion attempted, maintain therapeutic anticoagulation for at least 4 weeks post-procedure
Ongoing monitoring of LV function and mitral regurgitation
comparison: No prior TEE available for comparison.
